_comment: Golden Query Suite for HR-Pro. Production engagements derive their suite from Ch.1's AWR top-N queries (by db time). Each query carries an engine-specific SQL variant so the runner can replay the same logical workload on each engine.
_cross_engine_notes: Q01: 'Recent hires' uses SYSDATE-90 (Oracle DATE), DATEADD(day,-90,GETUTCDATE()) (MI DATETIME), CURRENT_DATE-90 (PG DATE). Because hire_date is a DATE column on all three engines (see Ch.3.5 hr_pro_schema.sql), the comparison reduces to date-vs-date and produces equal row counts. If your source stores hire_date as TIMESTAMP, the time-of-day boundary differs and Q01 may diverge by a few rows at the window edge.

Q04: 'Status by month' returns DATE on Oracle and MI (via TRUNC and DATEFROMPARTS) but TIMESTAMP(TZ) on PG (via date_trunc). Row counts match across engines; the runner's outcome-based comparison passes. A hash-based reconciler over the rendered output would fail because of the type difference -- worth knowing if you re-derive a reconcile step from these queries.
Q05: PK lookup uses positional placeholders -- :1 (Oracle), ? (MI / pyodbc), %s (PG / psycopg). pvr_runner.py dispatches per engine, so params travel uniformly.

queries.description: Count current hires in the last 90 days per department -- typical BI dashboard query.

sql.oracle: SELECT d.dept_code, COUNT(*) FROM hrpro.employee e JOIN hrpro.department d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id WHERE e.hire_date >= SYSDATE - 90 GROUP BY d.dept_code ORDER BY d.dept_code
sql.mssql: SELECT d.dept_code, COUNT(*) FROM dbo.employee e JOIN dbo.department d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id WHERE e.hire_date >= DATEADD(day, -90, GETUTCDATE()) GROUP BY d.dept_code ORDER BY d.dept_code
sql.pg: SELECT d.dept_code, COUNT(*) FROM hrpro.employee e JOIN hrpro.department d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id WHERE e.hire_date >= CURRENT_DATE - 90 GROUP BY d.dept_code ORDER BY d.dept_code

queries.description: MV_HEADCOUNT_ROLLUP equivalent -- direct query (not the MV). Validates the Gold/MV replacement still produces equal results.

sql.oracle: SELECT d.dept_code, COUNT(*) emp_count, AVG(eh.salary) avg_salary FROM hrpro.employee e JOIN hrpro.department d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id LEFT JOIN hrpro.employee_history eh ON eh.emp_id = e.emp_id AND eh.end_date IS NULL GROUP BY d.dept_code ORDER BY d.dept_code
sql.mssql: SELECT d.dept_code, COUNT(*) AS emp_count, AVG(eh.salary) AS avg_salary FROM dbo.employee e JOIN dbo.department d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id LEFT JOIN dbo.employee_history eh ON eh.emp_id = e.emp_id AND eh.end_date IS NULL GROUP BY d.dept_code ORDER BY d.dept_code
sql.pg: SELECT d.dept_code, COUNT(*) emp_count, AVG(eh.salary) avg_salary FROM hrpro.employee e JOIN hrpro.department d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id LEFT JOIN hrpro.employee_history eh ON eh.emp_id = e.emp_id AND eh.end_date IS NULL GROUP BY d.dept_code ORDER BY d.dept_code

queries.description: Range scan over partitioned employee_history -- tests target's pruning of the equivalent partitioned/indexed table.

sql.oracle: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM hrpro.employee_history WHERE effective_date BETWEEN TO_DATE('2024-01-01','YYYY-MM-DD') AND TO_DATE('2024-12-31','YYYY-MM-DD')
sql.mssql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dbo.employee_history WHERE effective_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
sql.pg: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM hrpro.employee_history WHERE effective_date BETWEEN DATE '2024-01-01' AND DATE '2024-12-31'

queries.description: Time-series rollup on payroll_run; exercises COUNT/SUM aggregation and date truncation.

sql.oracle: SELECT TRUNC(run_date, 'MM') AS month, status, COUNT(*) FROM hrpro.payroll_run GROUP BY TRUNC(run_date, 'MM'), status ORDER BY month, status
sql.mssql: SELECT DATEFROMPARTS(YEAR(run_date), MONTH(run_date), 1) AS month, status, COUNT(*) AS runs FROM dbo.payroll_run GROUP BY DATEFROMPARTS(YEAR(run_date), MONTH(run_date), 1), status ORDER BY month, status
sql.pg: SELECT date_trunc('month', run_date) AS month, status, COUNT(*) FROM hrpro.payroll_run GROUP BY date_trunc('month', run_date), status ORDER BY month, status

queries.description: Single-row lookup by primary key. The latency floor.

sql.oracle: SELECT emp_id, first_name, last_name, hire_date FROM hrpro.employee WHERE emp_id = :1
sql.mssql: SELECT emp_id, first_name, last_name, hire_date FROM dbo.employee WHERE emp_id = ?
sql.pg: SELECT emp_id, first_name, last_name, hire_date FROM hrpro.employee WHERE emp_id = %s
